title: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
author: James Silberman
orientation: Reports that messengers at the 2021 Southern Baptist Convention overrode the Resolutions Committee by an 80-90% vote to bring the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion before the full convention, and previews abolitionist answers to anticipated objections from SBC leaders.
audiences: SBC denominational leader / Resolutions Committee member
Curious newcomer to the abolitionist movement

questions.question: What procedural action did SBC messengers take on Tuesday, and how unusual was it?
questions.answer: Messengers voted to override the Resolutions Committee and force the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion onto the convention floor. According to Bill Ascol, this kind of override had not happened since 1979, signaling extraordinary grassroots support for abolition within the SBC.
questions.quote: First, according to Ascol, this is the first time that the Resolutions Committee has been overridden in this way since he began attending SBC Annual Meetings in 1979. This demonstrates the immense popular support within the SBC for abolition.

questions.question: How does the SBC normally decide which resolutions reach the floor, and what threshold is required to override that process?
questions.answer: Ordinarily the Resolutions Committee selects which resolutions are voted on, but messengers can move to override that choice and a two-thirds vote is required for the override motion to succeed.
questions.quote: Southern Baptists can file resolutions, but it is ordinarily the prerogative of the Committee to decide which resolutions are voted on by messengers. However, messengers can motion to override the Committee's decision. A two-thirds vote is needed for this sort of motion to pass.

questions.question: Why did the Resolutions Committee initially refuse to bring the abolition resolution forward?
questions.audience: SBC denominational leader / Resolutions Committee member
questions.answer: The Committee preferred Resolution #3, which framed the issue around defending the Hyde Amendment and blocking Medicaid funding of abortion providers, rather than the abolitionist call for justice to be immediately established.
questions.quote: While the Committee believes that the Southern Baptist Convention messengers would agree with some aspects of this resolution, the Committee believes Resolution #3 addresses these issues in the context of the current threats to the Hyde Amendment and related pro-life appropriations riders in the United States Congress.

questions.question: Who introduced the override motion, and what was the response from the floor?
questions.answer: Bill Ascol, pastor of Bethel Baptist Church in Owasso, Oklahoma, introduced the motion late in Tuesday's meeting, and an estimated 80-90% of messengers backed the override against the Committee's recommendation.
questions.quote: Near the end of Tuesday's meeting, abolitionist Bill Ascol – pastor of Bethel Baptist Church in Owasso, OK – motioned to override their decision. An astonishing eighty to ninety percent of messengers responded in support of the motion, over the objections of the Resolutions Committee.

questions.question: Why does the article call this vote historic beyond just procedural significance?
questions.answer: Because it marks the first time the largest Christian denomination in the United States has taken a step toward becoming a prophetic witness demanding the immediate abolition of preborn child-killing, rather than merely settling for incremental pro-life policy.
questions.quote: But second – and more importantly – this is the first time that the largest Christian denomination in the United States has ever taken a step toward being a prophetic witness in calling for the immediate abolition of the murder of preborn children.

questions.question: How does the resolution distinguish abolitionism from being merely 'pro-life'?
questions.answer: The promotional pamphlet acknowledges that Southern Baptists already produce pro-life statements, sermons, articles, and conferences but argues they have failed to act as a unified, prophetic voice demanding magistrates abolish abortion totally and immediately in Jesus' name.
questions.quote: Southern Baptists have not failed to be pro-life. We have pro-life statements. We have pro-life Sanctity of Life sermons. We have pro-life articles, videos, and conferences. But we have failed to be a unified, prophetic voice demanding of our magistrates the total and immediate abolition of abortion in Jesus' name.

questions.question: What three objections from SBC leaders does the article promise to answer?
questions.audience: SBC denominational leader / Resolutions Committee member
questions.answer: The article lists objections that women should be immune from prosecution as victims of abortion, that there must be a life-of-the-mother exception, and that abolitionists should accept incremental gains rather than push for total abolition.
questions.quote:
Objection #1: Women are victims of abortion and should be immune in all cases

##### Objection #2: What about the life of the mother exception?

##### Objection #3: We should take what we can get

questions.question: What is the abolitionist response to the 'take what we can get' incrementalist objection?
questions.answer: The article points readers to a presentation by abolitionist attorney Bradley Pierce arguing that every facet of the pro-life incrementalist strategy has failed.
questions.quote: Abolitionist attorney Bradley Pierce explains in this presentation how all seven facets of the pro-life incrementalist strategy have been utter failures.

questions.question: What framework does the article use to summarize the foundational beliefs of abolitionism?
questions.audience: Curious newcomer to the abolitionist movement
questions.answer: The Five Tenets of Abolitionism, captured by the acronym GATES: Gospel-centered, Aligned providentially, Through the Church, Engaged Biblically, and Sought immediately without exception or compromise.
questions.quote:
The five tenets are the foundations of our public theology and can be referred to by the acronym GATES. Abolitionism is:

1. Gospel-centered
2. Aligned providentially
3. Through the Church
4. Engaged Biblically
5. Sought immediately without exception or compromise

related.title: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
related.reason: Background on the very resolution that messengers voted to bring to the floor in this article.

related.title: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
related.reason: Directly follows the procedural override described here, reporting on what happened when the resolution actually came to a vote.

related.title: Prominent Southern Baptist Voice Calls For Total Abolition
related.reason: Earlier coverage of Bill Ascol publicly aligning with abolitionism, setting up his role as the override motion's sponsor.

related.title: Refuting Dana Hall McCain's Nonsensical Anti-Abolitionist Slander
related.reason: Continues James Silberman's work answering SBC-adjacent leaders who attacked the abolition resolution after this vote.

related.title: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
related.reason: Extends the article's promise to answer SBC objections by engaging high-profile pro-life ethicists who opposed the resolution.

related.title: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
related.reason: Provides longer-form historical context for the SBC abolitionist movement that produced this Tuesday override vote.

related.title: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
related.reason: Shows the institutional SBC opposition that abolitionists overcame to force the 2021 resolution onto the floor.
